reasoning_model_1: Model 1 demonstrates excellent content accuracy, matching the script perfectly. In terms of the core paralinguistic requirement—emphasizing the word '就' (jiu)—the model performs very well. At timestamp 00:00, the speaker initiates the sentence with a forceful and high-pitched delivery of '就', distinctively separating it from the subsequent text. This creates the intended contrastive stress ('I *just* don't believe...'). The prosody throughout the clip is determined and natural, with effective pauses (e.g., the slight separation before '有信心' at 00:09) that reinforce the confident tone. The instruction to control the stress feature is fully met.
reasoning_model_2: Model 2 achieves high content accuracy and generates fluent, natural-sounding speech. However, it fails to strictly follow the specific paralinguistic instruction to emphasize '就'. Listening to the segment at 00:00, the phrase '我就不相信' is delivered with a standard, flat declarative intonation. The word '就' receives no special auditory prominence in terms of loudness, pitch, or duration; instead, the natural rhythmic stress falls on '不' (bu). While the audio quality is good, the lack of control over the requested feature results in a generic delivery that ignores the user's specific command.
model_comparison: Both models succeed in generating fluent and accurate speech based on the text. The decisive difference lies in the 'Feature Control' dimension. Model 1 (T1) correctly implements the user's command to emphasize the word '就', modifying the intonation at the very beginning (00:00) to make that specific word stand out perceptibly. Model 2 (T2), while natural, treats the sentence with a default prosody where '就' is unstressed. Consequently, Model 1 is superior as it demonstrates the ability to control specific paralinguistic features upon request, whereas Model 2 ignores the constraint.
